title: He Pūrākau o te Taiao: The Living Narratives of Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore Ngāti Kahungunu mātauranga through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that gives our college its name. They will learn how our tūpuna used environmental indicators like tuna (eels), water quality, and riparian plants as living knowledge systems for navigation, resource management, and seasonal forecasting.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain three traditional Ngāti Kahungunu environmental indicators from Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and their contemporary relevance
Compare and contrast Māori and scientific knowledge systems through water quality testing and traditional observation methods
Create a digital story map documenting the cultural and ecological significance of specific locations along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Collaborate with local kaumātua to develop recommendations for stream restoration that honor both mātauranga Māori and modern conservation practices

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa
activities.description: Students begin at the college's stream entrance with a karakia and mihi to the awa. They observe and record initial impressions, then work with kaumātua to learn traditional names for stream features and identify significant plants like harakeke and toetoe that indicate water health.

activities.title: He Tohu o te Taiao
activities.description: At three designated sites along the stream, students employ both traditional observation methods (tuna migration patterns, water clarity, plant health) and scientific tools (pH testing, temperature gauges) to assess stream health, creating comparative data sets.

activities.title: Pūrākau Pēke
activities.description: Using tablets, students interview kaumātua and local experts about specific pūrākau (stories) associated with the stream. They gather audio, video, and images to create digital content that connects physical locations to cultural narratives.

activities.title: Ngā Kete Mātauranga
activities.description: In mixed groups, students analyze their collected data to identify connections and tensions between knowledge systems. They create visual representations showing how mātauranga Māori and science can work together for environmental management.

activities.title: Hui Whakatau
activities.description: Students present their findings and restoration recommendations to an authentic audience including kaumātua, local conservation group representatives, and college leadership in a formal hui setting, practicing tikanga appropriate for such gatherings.

resources: Kaumātua Hōri Waititi (Ngāti Kahungunu environmental knowledge holder)
Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: Cultural Mapping Project (local iwi database)
Water quality testing kits from Hawke's Bay Regional Council
Digital story mapping software (ArcGIS or Google Earth)

tasks: Digital story map documenting cultural and ecological findings
Comparative analysis report of traditional and scientific knowledge systems
Oral presentation of restoration recommendations to community panel

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L4: Understand how people pass on and sustain culture and heritage for different reasons and that this has consequences for people
Social Studies L4: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
Social Studies L4: Understand how the ways in which leadership of groups is acquired and exercised have consequences for communities and societies
Science L4: Living World: Ecology - Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes
